class Solution {

public:

    int numberOfStableArrays(int zero, int one, int limit) {

        const int MOD = 1e9 +7;

        // Create a 4D DP array.

        vector<vector<vector<vector<int>>>> dp(zero+1, vector<one+1, vector<2, vector<int>>(2, vector<int>(limit+2, 0))>);

        // Initialize.

        if (zero > 0) {

            dp[1][0][0][1] = 1;

        }

        if (one > 0) {

            dp[0][1][1][1] = 1;

        }

        for (int z = 0; z <= zero; ++z) {

            for (int o = 0; o <= one; ++o) {

                for (int d = 0; d < 2; ++d) {

                    for (int r = 1; r <= limit; ++r) {

                        if (dp[z][o][d][r] == 0) continue;

                        // Try adding 0.

                                if (new_r <= limit) {

                                    dp[z+1][o][0][new_r] = (dp[z+1][o][0][new_r] + dp[z][o][d][r]) % MOD;

                                }

                            } else {

                            }

                        }

                }

            }

        }

        int result =0;

        for (int d=0; d<2; ++d) {

            for (int r=1; r<= limit; ++r) {

                result = (result + dp[zero][one][d][r]) % MOD;

            }

        }

        return result;

    }

};

The space complexity is O(zero * one * 2 * limit) = 200*200*2*200 = 16,000,000 integers, which is about 64MB.

So, the code should work.

solution:
```cpp
#include <vector>

using namespace std;

class Solution {
public:
    int numberOfStableArrays(int zero, int one, int limit) {
        const int MOD = 1e9 + 7;
        vector<vector<vector<vector<int>>>> dp(zero + 1, vector<one + 1, vector<2, vector<int>>>(2, vector<int>(limit + 2, 0)));
        
        if (zero > 0) {
            dp[1][0][0][1] = 1;
        }
        if (one > 0) {
            dp[0][1][1][1] = 1;
        }
        
        for (int z = 0; z <= zero; ++z) {
            for (int o = 0; o <= one; ++o) {
                for (int d = 0; d < 2; ++d) {
                    for (int r = 1; r <= limit; ++r) {
                        if (dp[z][o][d][r] == 0) continue;
                        
                        // Try adding 0
                        if (z < zero) {
                            if (d == 0) {
                                int new_r = r + 1;
                                if (new_r <= limit) {
                                    dp[z + 1][o][0][new_r] = (dp[z + 1][o][0][new_r] + dp[z][o][d][r]) % MOD;
                                }
                            } else {
                                dp[z + 1][o][0][1] = (dp[z + 1][o][0][1] + dp[z][o][d][r]) % MOD;
                            }
                        }
                        
                        // Try adding 1
                        if (o < one) {
                            if (d == 1) {
                                int new_r = r + 1;
                                if (new_r <= limit) {
                                    dp[z][o + 1][1][new_r] = (dp[z][o + 1][1][new_r] + dp[z][o][d][r]) % MOD;
                                }
                            } else {
                                dp[z][o + 1][1][1] = (dp[z][o + 1][1][1] + dp[z][o][d][r]) % MOD;
                            }
                        }
                    }
                }
            }
        }
        
        int result = 0;
        for (int d = 0; d < 2; ++d) {
            for (int r = 1; r <= limit; ++r) {
                result = (result + dp[zero][one][d][r]) % MOD;
            }
        }
        return result;
    }
};
```

efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(zero * one * limit)
space complexity: O(zero * one * limit)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution uses dynamic programming to efficiently count the number of valid binary arrays by tracking the state of the last few elements and ensuring no subarray exceeds the limit. The approach ensures that each state transition is computed in constant time, leading to high efficiency.
